content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES C

August 2, 1995

Honorable Paul Bailey
Mayor, City of Pismo Beach
P.O. Box 50
Pismo Beach, CA 93448

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-230

Dear Mayor Bailey:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

As an owner of a building subject to the city's Unreinforced
Masonry Building Safety Code, may you vote on the repeal of the
code and the adoption of the California State Unreinforced Masonry
Building Code Ordinance in its place?

CONCLUSION

As an owner of a building subject to the city's Unreinforced
Masonry Building Safety Code, you may not vote on either the
repeal of the city's existing code or the adoption of the
California State Unreinforced Masonry Building Code Ordinance in
its place.

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-230
Page 2


FACTS²

You and your spouse own, in partnership, a hotel in the
jurisdiction of Pismo Beach. The hotel (and underlying property)
is worth $960,000. You stated that you lease one portion of the
hotel to another party. The leased property is used as a
restaurant.

Currently, the Pismo Beach City Council is considering the
repeal of the city's Unreinforced Masonry Building Safety Code.
The existing code requires that unreinforced masonry buildings
have all hazards repaired by January 1, 1997. In place of the
existing code, the city council will consider the adoption of the
California State Unreinforced Masonry Building Code Ordinance in
its place. Under the state code, owners of unreinforced masonry
buildings will have a greater period of time to make the required
structural changes.

Your hotel, along with thirteen other buildings in the city,
is subject to the Unreinforced Masonry Building Safety Code. The
city's population is approximately 7,800.

ANALYSIS

Economic Interest

The Act was adopted by the voters in California by initiative
in 1974. The purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in mak-
ing, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any
economic interest, including:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.


_________________________
2 These facts are based on your letters of July 10 and July 19,
1995, and our telephone conversation of July 26, 1995.

Our File No. A-95-230
Page 3


     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     In your letter, you described a variety of economic interests
that may result in disqualification:

Business Entities: Your July 19, 1995, letter indicates that you
and your spouse own, in partnership, a hotel in the jurisdiction
of Pismo Beach. Since your partnership is a profit-making
business, the partnership is an economic interest under Sections
87103(a) and (d).

Real Property: You also own the property on which you operate your
hotel business. This constitutes an interest in real property
pursuant to section 87103(b). You stated that the value of the
building and property is $960,000.

Sources of Income: You also stated that you lease space to a
tenant who uses a portion of the building as a restaurant. Thus,
the tenant is considered a source of income to you (presumably of
more than $250 in the past 12 months). (Section 87103(c).)

     Since each of these interests is considered an economic
interest, pursuant to section 87100 and 87103, you may not
participate in any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on these interests.

Foreseeability and Materiality

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the
facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-95-230
Page 4


You have asked specifically about the repeal of the city's
Unreinforced Masonry Building Safety Code and adoption of a new
code. Since the new code will alter the time frame in which you
must make repairs to your building, it is certain that the
decisions will have some effect on both your business and the real
property you own for the business.

However, for a foreseeable financial effect on your interests
to be disqualifying, the effect must also be material. With
respect to your business, Regulation 18702.2(g)³ (copy enclosed)
provides that the effect of a decision on your business is
material if:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Thus, if subdivision (g) is the appropriate standard, you
will have a conflict of interest if the decision will eliminate or
avoid $2,500 in expenses in a fiscal year. Since this appears to
be the case, absent some exception, you would not be able to
participate in either decision.⁴

"Public Generally" Exception

Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict-of-
interest provisions if the effect on the official's interest is
not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. For
the "public generally" exception to apply, a decision must affect
the official's interests in substantially the same manner as it

_______________
     ³ Regulation 18702.2 provides different dollar thresholds for
materiality depending on the financial size of the business
entity. Subdivisions (g) applies to relatively small businesses.
You should consult the regulation to determine whether subdivision
(g) is the appropriate standard.

     ⁴ You will also be disqualified from voting on the ordinance if
the decisions will materially affect the value of your property
and hotel. Regulation 18702.3(c) provides that you would have a
conflict of interest if the decision will either have a $10,000 or
greater effect on the fair market value of the real property, or
would effect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more
per 12 month period.

Our File No. A-95-230
Page 5


would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation
18703.) Regulation 18703 provides that the "public generally"
exception applies where both subdivisions (1) and (2) apply.

(1) Significant Segment: The governmental
decision will affect a "significant segment" of the
public generally as set forth below:

(A) The decision will affect:

(i) Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(ii) Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(iii) Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction of the
district the official represents, so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,

(B) The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,

* * *

(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The
governmental decision will affect the official's
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this
regulation.

You stated that only 14 buildings will be subject to either
the existing ordinance or the new ordinance. When compared to the
population of the city as a whole, or all property owners, all
home owners, all households or all businesses, the number of
affected persons does not reach any of the thresholds in the
regulation.

You asked specifically about Regulation 18703(a)(1)(D). This
provision is a very narrow exception that was adopted in
conjunction with subdivision (c) (applicable to states of
emergency) to apply in exceptional circumstances where large
numbers of persons were affected. Your facts do not reflect any
exceptional circumstances regarding the decision for it to fall

Our File No. A-95-230
Page 6


within this provision. Thus, the "public generally" exception
does not apply and you may not participate in the decision in
question.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.5

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

ENCLOSURES




5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
